Code fragment: public final TableField<TodolistRecord, Integer> ID = createField(DSL.name("id"), SQLDataType.INTEGER.nullable(false).identity(true), this, "");
Explanation: The primary key column is named just 'id', which is a generic name that doesn't convey any meaning about the table it belongs to. Consider renaming it to 'todolist_id' or 'todo_id' to make it more descriptive and avoid ambiguity in queries with joins.

Code fragment: public final TableField<TodolistRecord, Boolean> COMPLETED = createField(DSL.name("completed"), SQLDataType.BOOLEAN.defaultValue(DSL.field(DSL.raw("false"), SQLDataType.BOOLEAN)), this, "");
Explanation: The 'completed' column has a default value but is still marked as NULLABLE (no .nullable(false) specified). Since it has a default value of 'false', it likely should never be NULL in practice. Consider adding .nullable(false) to the column definition to enforce this constraint.

Code fragment: public final TableField<TodolistRecord, String> STATUS = createField(DSL.name("status"), SQLDataType.VARCHAR(10).defaultValue(DSL.field(DSL.raw("'DRAFT'::character varying"), SQLDataType.VARCHAR)), this, "");
Explanation: The 'status' column has a default value of 'DRAFT' but is still marked as NULLABLE. Since it has a meaningful default value, it likely should never be NULL in practice. Consider adding .nullable(false) to the column definition to enforce this constraint.

Using `select()` without specifying columns fetches all columns from the TODOLIST table, which can retrieve unnecessary data and hurt performance. Explicitly select only the needed columns: `dsl.select(TODOLIST.ID, TODOLIST.TITLE, TODOLIST.COMPLETED).from(TODOLIST).fetch()`.
